schools.summary: 'A true saying' (pistos ho logos, pointing forward) introduces the church officers; 'if a man desire the office of a bishop' (the overseer's office/eldership, to 'reach out after,' oregetai) is a commendable longing; 'he desireth a good work' — a 'work,' laborious and demanding, yet 'good,' excellent and useful (Wesley: excellent but laborious); the desire honourable, the requirements searching.

themes: if a man desire the office of a bishop

schools.summary: The overseer's (episkopos, used for elders, Acts 20:28; Titus 1:7) qualifications — 'blameless' (unblemished reputation, not sinless), 'husband of one wife' (marital fidelity, excluding polygamy; neither requiring marriage nor forbidding a second lawful one, Wesley), 'vigilant' (watchful), 'sober' (prudent), 'of good behaviour' (orderly), 'given to hospitality,' 'apt to teach' (the one gift, able to instruct in sound doctrine); character weighed more than gift.

debates.question: What does 'the husband of one wife' require?

sides.position: marital fidelity, a man of one wife at a time, excluding polygamy; not requiring marriage nor forbidding a second lawful marriage

schools.summary: The negative virtues — 'not given to wine' (me paroinon, not a tippler, nor made imperious by it, Clarke), 'no striker' (not violent), 'not greedy of filthy lucre' (not in ministry for sordid gain); the answering graces 'patient/gentle' (epieike, forbearing), 'not a brawler' (not quarrelsome), 'not covetous'; self-control and freedom from avarice adorning the office.

The domestic test — 'ruleth well his own house' (proistamenon, governing the family wisely, not by tyranny but with authority and dignity, Clarke); 'having his children in subjection with all gravity' (respectful, orderly children, with a dignity that commands respect; levity undermines domestic authority, Wesley); the well-ordered home the visible proof of fitness to govern the church.

themes: home a test for the church

schools.summary: The argument from lesser to greater — the home a small, simpler charge, the church of God a weightier one; if a man 'know not how to rule' (prostenai, know how) his own house, how shall he 'take care of the church of God'? A church resembles a family, only larger (Barnes); the rhetorical question expects 'he cannot' — failure at home disqualifies for the church.

themes: if a man rule not his own house
how take care of the church?

schools.summary: 'Not a novice' (neophyton, newly-planted, our 'neophyte,' a convert whose roots have not struck deep, lacking experience and stability); 'lest being lifted up with pride' (typhotheis, swollen with conceit, sudden elevation turning the head) 'he fall into the condemnation of the devil' — the very condemnation through pride into which the devil fell (Wesley); the office a snare to the unproven.

'A good report of them which are without' (outsiders, the heathen and the world, who can respect an upright life though rejecting the faith; Paul's concern for the witness before outsiders, 1 Thess 4:12; Col 4:5); 'lest he fall into reproach' (exposing him and the gospel to scorn) 'and the snare of the devil' (the trap by which Satan, through disgrace, overthrows him).

The deacons (diakonous, the second office, Phil 1:1) — whose work (Acts 6) was the church's temporal affairs, relieving the poor and serving tables; though not preachers, men of tested character — 'grave' (dignified), 'not doubletongued' (not deceitful or inconsistent in speech), 'not given to much wine,' 'not greedy of filthy lucre' (vital in those handling the church's money and charity).
Chrysostom: passing from bishops to deacons, omitting presbyters as no great difference from bishops.

'The mystery of the faith' (the revealed gospel truth, once hidden, now in Christ, 1 Cor 2:7) — the deacon, though not a preacher, firmly holding the great doctrines; 'in a pure conscience' (held with a clean conscience, a holy heart and life); the pure conscience the casket keeping the jewel of the faith; mere orthodoxy without the Christ-life empty (Ellicott).

'First be proved' (dokimazesthosan, tested as metals, 1 Thess 2:4) — character and fitness examined before appointment, not a formal public investigation but a trial over time (Ellicott); 'then let them use the office... being found blameless' — office the reward of proven fitness, not given to the untried; no man in church office till he has given full proof (Clarke).

Qualifications for these 'women' — 'grave' (dignified), 'not slanderers' (no malicious gossip), 'sober' (temperate), 'faithful in all things' (trustworthy toward God, husband, and the poor); the reference long debated.

sides.position: women serving as deaconesses (like Phoebe, Rom 16:1), the placement amid the deacon-rules favouring this
the wives of the deacons (the older rendering)

schools.summary: The deacons' domestic qualification, the same as the overseer's — 'the husbands of one wife' (mias gynaikos, marital fidelity, one wife at a time, as v2) and 'ruling their children and their own houses well' (proistamenoi kalos, as v4); the same orderly home required of the deacon as of the presiding elder (Ellicott), the household the proof of character for both.

The reward of faithful deacons — those who 'use the office well' (with diligence and sincerity) 'gain to themselves' (peripoiountai, lay up) a twofold reward: 'a good degree' (bathmon, a good standing/esteem, or a step toward higher office, Clarke/Wesley) and 'great boldness in the faith' (a holy confidence and assurance springing from a good conscience); diligent service rewarded with honour and inward boldness.

The occasion — 'these things write I' (the directions on overseers and deacons) 'hoping to come unto thee shortly' (en tachei); Paul intending to return to Ephesus in person, writing more briefly in that hope (Henry); the undesigned detail of a soon-return not realized bearing the mark of authentic correspondence a forger would not invent (Robertson).

The letter's aim — if Paul is delayed, Timothy must 'know how to behave' (order the church); three titles exalt it — 'the house of God' (his household and temple, ordered with reverence), 'the church of the living God' (belonging to the true, living God, not a dead idol), 'the pillar and ground of the truth' (the support and bulwark holding the truth up before the world); the church's glory motivating the careful ordering.

themes: how to behave in the house of God
the church of the living God
the pillar and ground of the truth

schools.summary: The great confession — 'without controversy' (homologoumenos, confessedly) 'great is the mystery of godliness,' the truth the church upholds; a six-fold hymn/creed summing Christ's whole economy — 'manifest in the flesh' (incarnation; textual question 'God' or 'who,' the incarnate Christ either way), 'justified in the Spirit' (vindicated, esp. in resurrection), 'seen of angels,' 'preached unto the Gentiles,' 'believed on in the world,' 'received up into glory' (ascension); incarnation to glorification, the sum of the gospel.

debates.question: Did the text read 'God' or 'who' was manifest in the flesh?

sides.position: a textual question between 'God' (theos) and 'who/which' (hos/ho); the sense of the incarnate Christ stands either way
